title: Pozole Rojo Red Hominy Soup

seo.title: Authentic Pozole Rojo Recipe | Mexican Hominy Soup
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make the best authentic Pozole Rojo recipe at home. This hearty Mexican hominy soup with tender pork is perfect for any occasion. Get the step-by-step guide!
seo.h1: Authentic Pozole Rojo (Mexican Red Hominy Soup)
h2Headings: Pro Tips for the Best Results
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of authentic Pozole Rojo, a hearty Mexican red hominy soup with tender pork, garnished with radish, cabbage, and lime.
seo.imageCaption: Warm up with a bowl of this incredibly flavorful and authentic Pozole Rojo, a traditional Mexican red hominy soup perfect for any celebration!

description: Pozole Rojo is a beloved Mexican classic, a rich and hearty red hominy soup known for its tender pork and vibrant chili broth. This comforting dish is a staple for celebrations and family gatherings, offering a perfect blend of savory flavors and satisfying textures. It is traditionally garnished with crisp cabbage, radishes, onion, and a squeeze of fresh lime.

ingredients.notes: half chopped, half thinly sliced for garnish

instructions.title: Prepare Pork and Chiles
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat vegetable oil over medium-high heat. Add pork shoulder cubes and sear until lightly browned on all sides, about 5-7 minutes. Add half of the chopped white onion and half of the minced garlic to the pot, cooking until fragrant, about 2 minutes. Meanwhile, place dried guajillo and ancho chiles in a small bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 10-15 minutes until softened.

instructions.title: Simmer Pork Base
instructions.text: Pour 4 cups of chicken broth or water into the pot with the seared pork. Add the bay leaf and dried oregano. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 15 minutes to tenderize the pork slightly and build flavor.

instructions.title: Blend the Chili Sauce
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving a little soaking liquid. Transfer the softened chiles to a blender along with the remaining half of the chopped white onion, remaining minced garlic, cumin powder, and 1/2 cup of fresh chicken broth. Blend until completely smooth. If the sauce is too thick, add a tablespoon or two of the reserved chile soaking liquid or more broth until it reaches a pourable consistency.

instructions.title: Combine and Simmer
instructions.text: Carefully pour the blended chili sauce through a fine-mesh sieve directly into the pot with the simmering pork, pressing on the solids to extract all the liquid. Discard any remaining solids in the sieve. Add the rinsed and drained hominy to the pot along with the remaining 2 cups of chicken broth. Stir everything together.

instructions.title: Finish Cooking
instructions.text: Bring the pozole to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover, and let it simmer for an additional 20 minutes. This allows the flavors to meld and the hominy to absorb the delicious broth. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt as needed.

instructions.title: Serve with Garnishes
instructions.text: Remove the bay leaf from the pot before serving. Ladle the hot Pozole Rojo into individual bowls. Serve immediately with a generous array of traditional garnishes on the side, such as shredded green cabbage, thinly sliced radishes, thinly sliced white onion, and fresh lime wedges. Offer tostadas or tortilla chips for dipping.

tips: For richer flavor, toast the dried chiles lightly in a dry pan for 1-2 minutes before rehydrating them. Be careful not to burn them.
Always pass your blended chili sauce through a fine-mesh sieve. This step ensures a smooth, restaurant-quality broth without any bits of chile skin.
Pozole is even better the next day! Prepare it in advance to allow the flavors to deepen. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
Embrace the vibrant flavors and rich culinary heritage of Mexico with a steaming bowl of Pozole Rojo. This iconic red hominy soup is more than just a meal; it's a celebration, a comfort, and a testament to generations of Mexican cooking. Perfect for chilly evenings, family gatherings, or simply a hearty craving, Pozole Rojo offers a symphony of savory pork, tender hominy, and a deeply flavorful chile broth that will warm you from the inside out.

## What is Pozole Rojo?

At its heart, Pozole Rojo is a hearty Mexican stew characterized by its use of nixtamalized corn kernels, known as hominy, and a robust red broth made from dried chiles. The word "pozole" itself comes from the Nahuatl word "pozolli," meaning "hominy." While there are several variations, Pozole Rojo typically features tender pieces of pork (though chicken is also common), simmered for hours until incredibly tender. The distinctive red color and depth of flavor come from a blend of rehydrated and blended dried chiles like guajillo, ancho, and sometimes a touch of spicy árbol.

This beloved dish holds a special place in Mexican culture, often served during holidays and celebrations such as Mexican Independence Day, Christmas, and New Year's Eve. It's a dish meant for sharing, bringing families and friends together around a steaming pot, each person customizing their bowl with an array of fresh garnishes. The process of making Pozole Rojo can be a labor of love, but the deeply satisfying aroma and incredible taste make every moment worthwhile.

## Ingredients You'll Need

Crafting an authentic Pozole Rojo requires a few key ingredients, each playing a crucial role in building its complex flavor profile.

For the Pork:
*   2-3 pounds pork shoulder (Boston butt) or pork hock, cut into 2-inch chunks
*   1 large white onion, halved
*   4-6 cloves garlic, peeled
*   2 bay leaves
*   1 tablespoon salt
*   Water to cover

For the Red Chile Broth:
*   8-10 dried guajillo chiles, stems and seeds removed
*   3-4 dried ancho chiles, stems and seeds removed
*   2-3 dried árbol chiles (optional, for heat), stems and seeds removed
*   2 cloves garlic
*   1/2 white onion
*   1 teaspoon dried Mexican oregano
*   1/2 teaspoon ground cumin
*   1/4 cup reserved pork cooking liquid (or chicken broth)

For the Hominy:
*   2 (25-ounce) cans large white hominy, drained and rinsed, or 1 pound dried hominy (prepared according to package directions)

For Serving (Garnishes):
*   Shredded cabbage or finely shredded lettuce
*   Thinly sliced radishes
*   Diced white onion
*   Chopped fresh cilantro
*   Lime wedges
*   Sliced avocado
*   Tostadas or tortilla chips
*   Mexican crema or sour cream (optional)
*   Dried Mexican oregano (for sprinkling)

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Making Pozole Rojo is a process of layering flavors. Follow these steps for a delicious and authentic result:

1.  **Cook the Pork:** Place the pork chunks in a large pot or Dutch oven. Add the halved onion, whole garlic cloves, bay leaves, and salt. Cover with water by about 2 inches. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 2-3 hours, or until the pork is fork-tender. Skim off any foam or impurities that rise to the surface during the first hour. Once cooked, remove the pork from the broth, reserving about 1/4 cup of the cooking liquid for the chile sauce. Shred or chop the pork once it's cool enough to handle. Discard the onion, garlic, and bay leaves from the broth, but strain and reserve the broth itself.

2.  **Prepare the Chiles:** While the pork cooks, prepare your chiles. Heat a dry skillet or comal over medium heat. Toast the dried guajillo, ancho, and árbol chiles for 30 seconds to 1 minute per side, just until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the sauce bitter. Remove from heat and place the toasted chiles in a bowl. Cover them with hot water and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes, until softened.

3.  **Make the Red Chile Broth:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, discarding the soaking water. Transfer the softened chiles to a blender. Add the 2 cloves of garlic, 1/2 white onion, Mexican oregano, ground cumin, and the reserved 1/4 cup of pork cooking liquid (or chicken broth). Blend until completely smooth. If the mixture is too thick, add a little more strained pork broth, a tablespoon at a time, until it reaches a pourable consistency.

4.  **Strain the Chile Sauce:** For a silky-smooth broth, strain the chile sauce through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl, pressing down on the solids with the back of a spoon to extract all the liquid. Discard the remaining pulp. This step is crucial for achieving the characteristic texture of Pozole Rojo.

5.  **Combine and Simmer:** In a clean large pot or Dutch oven, add the strained pork broth. Bring it to a gentle simmer. Stir in the strained red chile sauce. Add the shredded pork and the drained and rinsed hominy. Bring the mixture back to a gentle simmer.

6.  **Final Simmer and Seasoning:** Cook for another 30-45 minutes, allowing all the flavors to meld together. Taste and adjust seasoning as needed, adding more salt if necessary. The broth should be rich, savory, and slightly spicy.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

*   **Quality Chiles are Key:** The flavor of your Pozole Rojo hinges on the quality and freshness of your dried chiles. Source them from a reputable Mexican grocery store.
*   **Toast, Don't Burn:** When toasting chiles, a few seconds can make all the difference. Toasting brings out their smoky flavor, but burning them will introduce a bitter taste.
*   **Strain for Smoothness:** Don't skip straining the chile sauce. It removes any tough skin or seed fragments, resulting in a beautifully smooth, restaurant-quality broth.
*   **Slow and Low:** Cooking the pork slowly and gently ensures it becomes incredibly tender and flavorful. This long simmer also develops the base for your broth.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** Seasoning is personal. Always taste your Pozole Rojo and adjust salt, and even a pinch more oregano or cumin, until it's perfect for your palate. A squeeze of lime at the end brightens all the flavors.
*   **Make Ahead:** Pozole Rojo often tastes even better the next day, after the flavors have had more time to meld. It's a great make-ahead meal for parties.

## Serving Suggestions

The beauty of Pozole Rojo lies not just in the soup itself, but in the vibrant array of garnishes that accompany it. These fresh toppings add contrasting textures, flavors, and a burst of freshness that elevates every spoonful.

Set up a "pozole bar" with small bowls of:
*   **Shredded Cabbage or Lettuce:** Adds a refreshing crunch.
*   **Diced White Onion & Cilantro:** Classic aromatic toppings.
*   **Thinly Sliced Radishes:** Offers a peppery bite and beautiful color.
*   **Lime Wedges:** Essential for a bright, tangy finish.
*   **Sliced Avocado:** Adds a creamy richness.
*   **Mexican Oregano:** A sprinkle over the top enhances the authentic flavor.
*   **Tostadas or Tortilla Chips:** For scooping up the delicious broth and hominy.

Guests can customize their bowls to their liking, creating a personalized experience with each serving of Pozole Rojo. Some also enjoy a drizzle of Mexican crema or sour cream for added richness, or a dash of hot sauce for an extra kick.

## Variations & Substitutions

While Pozole Rojo is a beloved classic, there are several delicious variations and substitutions you can explore:

*   **Pozole Verde:** Instead of red chiles, this version uses green chiles (like tomatillos, jalapeños, and poblanos) and often includes ingredients like pumpkin seeds, cilantro, and epazote for a bright, tangy, and herbal broth. It's typically made with chicken.
*   **Pozole Blanco:** This "white" pozole has a clear broth, seasoned simply with salt, garlic, and onion, allowing the natural flavor of the hominy and meat to shine. The color and flavor primarily come from the garnishes added at the table.
*   **Meat Options:** While pork is traditional for Pozole Rojo, you can easily substitute it with chicken (thighs or a whole chicken), or even a combination of pork and chicken. For a vegetarian option, use vegetable broth and add extra vegetables like zucchini, carrots, or mushrooms.
*   **Hominy:** If you can't find canned large white hominy, dried hominy is an excellent (and often preferred) option, though it requires pre-soaking and cooking.
*   **Chile Blends:** Experiment with different dried chiles to adjust the flavor and heat. For more heat, add more árbol chiles or a few cascabel chiles. For a smokier flavor, include a chipotle chile.

Making your own Pozole Rojo at home is a rewarding culinary journey that connects you to the heart of Mexican cuisine. With its comforting warmth, intricate flavors, and endless customization options, it’s a dish sure to become a cherished favorite in your kitchen.
